title: Heatwave fuels frustration in Paris's northern suburbs: 'If they'd just given us shutters, we wouldn't be suffering like this'
subhed: Over the past week, daily life in two large concrete housing complexes in Seine-Saint-Denis has changed dramatically due to extreme heat. Residents share their struggles and anger as they try to help each other cope.

title: Jean-Pierre Farandou, French labor minister: 'We cannot bring France to a halt every time it hits 30°C'

Russia-annexed Crimea declares 'emergency' amid Ukraine strikes and closes the beach season
subhed: Announcement comes amid fuel shortages and power cuts triggered by the Ukrainian attacks on logistics chains and oil facilities across Crimea, the rest of Russian-occupied Ukraine and southern Russia.

title: Lebanon, Israel, US sign trilateral framework agreement in Washington
subhed: The agreement comes after five rounds of talks aimed at ending hostilities between Israel and Hezbollah in southern Lebanon. It includes a pilot effort for Lebanese soldiers to take control of two areas occupied by Israel, as well as a process aimed at disarming Hezbollah.

title: Mansplashing: Why do men splash everyone at the pool?
subhed: The behavior of the so-called 'pool man,' who thrashes around in the water and sprawls out across swimming lanes, highlights the power dynamics at play in the shared social space of the public swimming pool.

title: 250 years of American independence: When John Adams predicted that democracy would commit suicide
subhed: 'America 250' (5/13). John Adams, the second president of the United States, and his successor Thomas Jefferson did not share the same view of human nature. Adams, an educated anti-slavery advocate from the North, feared that democracy would perish under the weight of corruption among the powerful. Posterity, however, has largely remembered the soaring rhetoric of Jefferson, the slaveholding Southern planter.

subhed: 'Donbas: The War of Attrition' (1/2). Just a few kilometers from one of Ukraine's fiercest fronts, these two cities are under drone strikes that make residents' lives very difficult. Both civilians and soldiers fear the bombings will intensify.

title: How cities in Africa are adapting to intense and deadly heat
subhed: Reflective paints, green roofs, work breaks: Many African countries are seeking innovative ways to cope with increasingly extreme temperatures.

title: 'Impossible to sleep': A night in sweltering France, in search of a cool spot
subhed: With a heatwave bearing down, the French have taken to living after dark, chasing relief from the sun's lingering grip. From Paris to Saint-Malo, by way of Orléans, parks and pools stirred to life as soon as the day ended.
